subject: ENGLISH
title: Common or Proper?
introduction: A common noun names a general person, place, animal, or thing. A proper noun names a specific one and begins with a capital letter. You will explore examples, sort cards, practice, and answer five questions.

vocabulary.definition: A key word used in the lesson “Common or Proper?”.
A key word used in the lesson “Common or Proper?”.
A key word used in the lesson “Common or Proper?”.

activities.instruction: Study the idea and listen to the narration.
activities.content: A common noun names a general person, place, animal, or thing. A proper noun names a specific one and begins with a capital letter.
activities.accessibilityAlternative: Text alternative: A common noun names a general person, place, animal, or thing. A proper noun names a specific one and begins with a capital letter.

activities.instruction: Explore each learning token, then read the examples.
activities.accessibilityAlternative: A numbered list of the same examples is available for keyboard and screen-reader use.

activities.instruction: Sort each card into “fits the lesson” or “does not fit.”

doesNotFit: Writing every common noun with a capital
Calling “happy” a noun in every sentence

feedback.correct: Your groups follow the lesson rule.
feedback.retry: Use the explanation, not color or position.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: Tap an item and then tap a labeled category; dragging is optional.

feedback.retry: Read the lesson rule again. island and teacher do not fit.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: All choices are visible text and selectable with TalkBack.

activities.instruction: Match each example to the lesson idea.

feedback.retry: Return to the explanation and compare one pair at a time.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: A list-based matching control replaces drag gestures.

activities.instruction: Complete the guided review in order.

feedback.correct: You are ready for the five-question check.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: Up and down buttons provide an alternative to dragging.

items.prompt: Which statement best matches “Common or Proper?”?

options.text: A common noun names a general person, place, animal, or thing. A proper noun names a specific one and begins with a capital letter.
Writing every common noun with a capital

items.explanation: The correct response is “A common noun names a general person, place, animal, or thing. A proper noun names a specific one and begins with a capital letter.” because it follows the lesson explanation: A common noun names a general person, place, animal, or thing. A proper noun names a specific one and begins with a capital letter.

options.text: Writing every common noun with a capital

items.explanation: The correct response is “city / Cebu City” because it follows the lesson explanation: A common noun names a general person, place, animal, or thing. A proper noun names a specific one and begins with a capital letter.

items.prompt: Which choice does not follow the lesson idea?

options.text: Writing every common noun with a capital

items.explanation: The correct response is “Writing every common noun with a capital” because it follows the lesson explanation: A common noun names a general person, place, animal, or thing. A proper noun names a specific one and begins with a capital letter.

The correct response is “Palawan” because it follows the lesson explanation: A common noun names a general person, place, animal, or thing. A proper noun names a specific one and begins with a capital letter.

items.prompt: Which example should be used in the final review?

options.text: Calling “happy” a noun in every sentence
Writing every common noun with a capital

items.explanation: The correct response is “river / Pasig River” because it follows the lesson explanation: A common noun names a general person, place, animal, or thing. A proper noun names a specific one and begins with a capital letter.

assetSpecs.prompt: Friendly Grade 3 cartoon storybook concept board for Common or Proper?; rounded shapes, warm dark-brown outlines, tropical palette, no embedded text, uncluttered small-screen layout. Compose overlays provide labels and interactive targets.
